questions.question: Which of the following is considered an established risk factor for colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Advanced age, especially over 50 years of age, is a well-documented risk factor for colorectal cancer, associated with accumulation of mutations.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism links tobacco and colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Tobacco contains carcinogens that induce oxidative stress and damage to the DNA of colon epithelial cells, promoting oncogenic mutations.

questions.question: What metabolic alteration associated with obesity increases the risk of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Obesity generates insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia; Insulin acts as a tumor growth factor in the colon mucosa.

questions.question: What is the recommended average age to start colorectal cancer screening in an average-risk population?
questions.explanation: International guidelines, such as those of the USMSTF, recommend starting screening at age 50 in asymptomatic people without additional risk factors.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of colorectal cancer is directly attributed to smoking?
questions.explanation: Epidemiological studies indicate that between 10% and 15% of colorectal cancer cases are related to tobacco consumption.

questions.question: What is the main inflammatory mediator linked to obesity that promotes colorectal carcinogenesis?
questions.explanation: TNF-alpha, a proinflammatory cytokine elevated in obesity, promotes chronic inflammation and cell proliferation in the colonic mucosa.

questions.question: What is the recommended age to start colorectal cancer screening in an average-risk population?
questions.explanation: In an average-risk population, international guidelines establish that colorectal cancer screening should begin at age 50, with colonoscopy every 10 years.

questions.question: What screening test is recommended every 10 years for colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is the screening test that is performed every 10 years, as it allows the visualization and removal of adenomatous polyps, reducing the incidence of colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What is the main objective of screening with colonoscopy every 10 years?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy screening seeks to detect and remove adenomatous polyps before they turn into cancer, thus preventing the development of colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What is the recommended interval between screening colonoscopies at average risk?
questions.explanation: For average risk population with normal colonoscopy, the recommended interval is 10 years, based on the slow progression of adenomatous polyps to carcinoma.

questions.question: What colonoscopic finding justifies shortening the screening interval to 3-5 years?
questions.explanation: Advanced adenoma (≥1 cm, with villous histology or high-grade dysplasia) requires more frequent surveillance, every 3-5 years, due to its higher risk of malignant progression.

questions.question: When can colorectal cancer screening be stopped at average risk?
questions.explanation: It is recommended to stop screening at age 75 in average-risk patients, considering the risk-benefit ratio and life expectancy.

questions.question: What is the most frequently mutated gene in Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome is due to mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes, with MLH1 being the most commonly affected gene, followed by MSH2.

questions.question: What type of inheritance does familial adenomatous polyposis present?
questions.explanation: Familial adenomatous polyposis follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, caused by mutations in the APC gene, with high penetrance and high risk of colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What is the surgical treatment of choice for familial adenomatous polyposis?
questions.explanation: In familial adenomatous polyposis, total colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis is the standard surgical treatment to prevent colorectal cancer, although proctocolectomy is considered depending on the number of rectal polyps.

questions.question: What is the main clinical criterion to suspect Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: The Amsterdam II criteria are the clinical standard for suspecting Lynch syndrome, including at least three relatives with colorectal cancer or associated tumors, one under 50 years of age.

questions.question: What gene is classically associated with familial adenomatous polyposis?
questions.explanation: Familial adenomatous polyposis is caused by germline mutations in the APC gene, located on chromosome 5q21, which regulates cell proliferation and apoptosis in the colonic epithelium.

questions.question: What is the most common extracolonic neoplasia in Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Endometrial cancer is the most common extracolonic neoplasm in Lynch syndrome, with a cumulative risk of 40-60%, so gynecological surveillance is recommended in female carriers.

questions.question: What is the most common anatomical location of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Colorectal cancer is most frequently located in the rectum and sigmoid, representing approximately 60% of cases, due to physiological factors and exposure to fecal carcinogens.

questions.question: What symptom is most characteristic of rectal and sigma cancer?
questions.explanation: Rectal bleeding and tenesmus are typical symptoms of rectal and sigmoid cancer due to their distal location, where the tumors bleed easily and generate a sensation of incomplete evacuation.

questions.question: Which diagnostic test is the most sensitive to detect rectal and sigmoid cancer?
questions.explanation: Total colonoscopy is the most sensitive test to detect colorectal cancer, since it allows you to directly visualize the rectal and sigmoid mucosa and take biopsies of suspicious lesions.

questions.question: What is the surgical treatment of choice for low rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Abdominoperineal amputation is the surgical treatment of choice for low rectal cancer, as it allows complete resection of the tumor and anal sphincter when preservation is not possible.

questions.question: Which risk factor is most strongly associated with rectal and sigma cancer?
questions.explanation: The consumption of processed red meat is a well-established risk factor for colorectal cancer, especially in the rectum and sigma, due to the formation of N-nitroso compounds and heterocyclic amines.

questions.question: What tumor marker is most frequently used in the monitoring of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is the most used tumor marker in the monitoring of colorectal cancer, especially to detect recurrences in patients with rectal and sigmoid tumors.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Bright red rectal bleeding is the most common clinical manifestation of rectal cancer, due to the distal location of the tumor and its tendency to bleed with the passage of stool.

questions.question: What alteration in bowel habit suggests left colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Constipation of recent or progressive onset is characteristic of left colon cancer, since the tumor narrows the lumen of the distal colon, making it difficult for fecal contents to advance.

questions.question: What is the initial screening test for colorectal cancer in the average-risk population?
questions.explanation: Immunochemical fecal occult blood is the recommended initial screening test in the average-risk population, due to its low cost, non-invasiveness, and ability to detect microscopic bleeding before obvious symptoms.

questions.question: Which finding on digital rectal examination is most suggestive of rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Palpation of a hard, irregular, fixed mass on the rectal wall is highly suggestive of rectal cancer, since rectal tumors usually present as infiltrative and painless lesions.

questions.question: What accompanying symptom worsens the prognosis in a patient with rectal bleeding due to cancer?
questions.explanation: Involuntary weight loss is a constitutional symptom that indicates advanced disease or metastasis, being associated with a worse prognosis in colorectal cancer, unlike localized symptoms such as isolated rectal bleeding.

questions.question: What is the first cause of iron deficiency anemia in older men?
questions.explanation: Right colon cancer is the first cause of iron deficiency anemia in older men because right tumors bleed chronically and occultly, without altering bowel habits or producing visible bleeding.

questions.question: What is the most common clinical presentation of right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Right colon cancer usually manifests with chronic iron deficiency anemia due to occult bleeding, since the fecal contents are liquid and do not cause early obstruction.

questions.question: Which laboratory study is usually altered in iron deficiency anemia due to right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia presents with low ferritin due to depletion of iron stores, being a key marker in chronic bleeding from right colon cancer.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains anemia in right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Chronic occult bleeding from the neoplasm consumes iron reserves, leading to iron deficiency anemia due to continuous blood loss without obvious digestive symptoms.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial imaging test in suspected right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Total colonoscopy allows you to directly visualize the mucosa of the right colon and take biopsies, being the initial diagnostic standard when colorectal neoplasia is suspected.

questions.question: Which colonoscopy finding suggests right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: An ulcerated or vegetative mass in the right colon is characteristic of adenocarcinoma, often associated with occult bleeding and iron deficiency anemia.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for localized right colon cancer?
questions.explanation: Right hemicolectomy with regional lymphadenectomy is the curative surgical treatment for localized right colon cancer, resecting the tumor and lymph nodes.

questions.question: In TNM staging of colorectal cancer, what does T2 mean?
questions.explanation: T2 in the TNM classification of colorectal cancer indicates that the tumor invades the muscularis propria, without crossing it into the subserosa or visceral peritoneum.

questions.question: What number of metastatic lymph nodes defines N2a in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: N2a in the TNM classification for colorectal cancer corresponds to metastases in 4 to 6 regional lymph nodes, according to the AJCC staging system.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of lymph nodes recommended for adequate staging in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: International guidelines recommend examining at least 12 lymph nodes in the colorectal cancer surgical specimen for accurate and prognostic nodal staging.

questions.question: In the TNM system, what stage corresponds to T3N1M0 in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: T3N1M0 is classified as stage IIIB in colorectal cancer, since it implies a tumor that crosses the muscularis propria without invading organs (T3) with metastases in 1-3 lymph nodes (N1) and without distant metastases (M0).

questions.question: What anatomical structure defines the difference between T3 and T4a in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: T4a is defined by invasion of the visceral peritoneum, while T3 is a tumor that passes through the muscularis propria without reaching the peritoneum; the serosa is the key boundary.

questions.question: What prognostic implication does the presence of tumor deposits without residual lymph node have in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Tumor deposits without evidence of residual lymph node are classified as N1c in the TNM system, which implies a prognosis similar to metastatic lymph node involvement.

questions.question: What is the main clinical utility of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: CEA is primarily used to monitor recurrence after surgical resection of colorectal cancer; It is not useful for diagnosis or screening due to its low sensitivity and specificity.

questions.question: What serum CEA level is generally considered elevated in a patient with colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The normal reference value is usually <5 ng/mL in non-smokers; Levels > 5 ng/mL are considered high and may suggest tumor presence or recurrence.

questions.question: What non-tumor condition can falsely elevate CEA in a patient under follow-up?
questions.explanation: Active smoking is a known cause of elevated CEA in the absence of neoplasia, which can generate false positives during oncological follow-up.

questions.question: How often is it recommended to measure CEA during the follow-up of stage II or III colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: The guidelines recommend measuring CEA every 3 to 6 months during the first two to three years after surgery, to detect recurrence early.

questions.question: Which finding on the CEA curve strongly suggests recurrence of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: A persistent or progressive elevation of CEA during follow-up, especially after it has fallen to normal levels, is highly suggestive of tumor recurrence.

questions.question: What adverse prognostic factor is associated with very high preoperative CEA levels?
questions.explanation: Very high preoperative levels of CEA (> 10 ng/mL) are associated with a worse prognosis and greater risk of recurrence, regardless of the pathological stage.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended extent of lymphadenectomy in colectomy for curative colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Standard oncologic lymphadenectomy for colorectal cancer requires resection of at least 12 lymph nodes for adequate staging and prognosis.

questions.question: What vascular structure is ligated first in an oncological left colectomy?
questions.explanation: In oncologic left colectomy, the left colic artery is first ligated to ensure resection of the regional lymphatic drainage of the descending colon.

questions.question: What is the main objective of D3 lymphadenectomy in colon cancer?
questions.explanation: D3 lymphadenectomy extends the resection to the central nodes, improving staging and overall survival by eliminating regional lymphatic metastases.

questions.question: What type of anastomosis is preferred after an oncological right hemicolectomy?
questions.explanation: The side-lateral ileocolic anastomosis is the preferred technique after right hemicolectomy, due to a lower rate of anastomotic leak and better functionality.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended proximal margin in a rectal cancer resection?
questions.explanation: In rectal cancer, a proximal margin of at least 2 cm of healthy tissue is recommended to ensure complete resection of the tumor and reduce local recurrence.

questions.question: What surgical technique allows preserving sphincter function in distal rectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Low anterior resection (LAR) with coloanal anastomosis preserves the anal sphincter in selected distal rectal tumors, avoiding permanent colostomy.

questions.question: What is the standard curative treatment for colorectal liver metastases?
questions.explanation: Complete surgical resection (R0) is the only potentially curative treatment for liver metastases from colorectal cancer, offering long-term survival.

questions.question: What neoadjuvant chemotherapy is used before liver resection?
questions.explanation: FOLFOX (oxaliplatin, 5-FU, leucovorin) or FOLFIRI (irinotecan, 5-FU, leucovorin) are standard neoadjuvant regimens to reduce liver metastases and allow resection.

questions.question: What criterion contraindicates curative liver resection?
questions.explanation: The presence of multiple, unresectable bilobar metastases contraindicate curative resection, since it is not possible to achieve an R0 resection with sufficient remaining parenchyma.

questions.question: What is the main goal of perioperative chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: Perioperative (neoadjuvant) chemotherapy seeks to reduce the size of liver metastases to facilitate complete surgical resection and improve survival.

questions.question: What finding contraindicates liver resection in metastasis?
questions.explanation: Invasion of the portal vein or the greater hepatic vein contraindicates curative resection, as it implies locally advanced and unresectable disease with a high risk of recurrence.

questions.question: What biological agent is combined with chemotherapy in liver metastases?
questions.explanation: Bevacizumab, an anti-VEGF antibody, is combined with 5-FU-based chemotherapy for colorectal liver metastases, improving response rates and resectability.

questions.question: What is the recommended interval for the first surveillance colonoscopy after curative resection of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: After curative resection of colorectal cancer, the first surveillance colonoscopy is recommended after one year to detect recurrences or new polyps.

questions.question: What tumor marker is routinely used for post-treatment monitoring of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is the standard tumor marker to monitor recurrence in patients with resected colorectal cancer.

questions.question: When should the first CEA determination be requested after curative surgery for colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: After curative surgery, CEA is measured every 3 to 6 months for the first 2-3 years, with every 3 months for 2 years being a common schedule.

questions.question: What finding on surveillance colonoscopy warrants a shortened repeat study?
questions.explanation: An advanced adenoma (≥10 mm, villous or high-grade dysplasia) requires more frequent colonoscopic follow-up, usually at 1 year.

questions.question: What is the response to a high CEA during follow-up without colonoscopy findings?
questions.explanation: In case of elevated CEA without lesions on colonoscopy, chest and abdominal CT is recommended to rule out distant metastasis.

questions.question: How long is it recommended to continue surveillance with CEA and colonoscopy after curative resection?
questions.explanation: Intensive surveillance with CEA and colonoscopy is recommended for at least 5 years after curative resection, although the risk persists beyond that.
